chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: The group is in the initial phase of introducing their respective research areas and brainstorming potential intersections, with no attempt to narrow down to a single project yet.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Participants are actively listening, with frequent head nods and audible backchannels (e.g., 'mm-hmm') observed during speaker turns.
chunk_summary.cross_disciplinary_bridging_description: Connects gut microbiome composition to quantitative imaging biomarkers in the brain.
chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: Participants are describing their own research domains and expressing interest in potential overlaps, but have not yet defined a specific research question.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: The group is still in the early stages of sharing expertise and identifying potential areas of interest.
chunk_summary.complementarity_recognition_quote: Perhaps how our tools and our expertise can play a role.
chunk_summary.shared_vision_quote: How we can drive the development of neuroimaging biomarkers of disease.
chunk_summary.notable_observation: The group successfully transitioned from individual introductions to identifying a shared interest in the gut-brain axis, with participants actively looking for ways to integrate their diverse expertise.

codes.evidence: I use a lot of rodent models, but we also do some work in breast cancer patients.

codes.evidence: I am trained as a mass spectrometrist... my lab primarily does proteomic and lipidomic profiling.

codes.evidence: Perhaps how our tools and our expertise can play a role.

codes.evidence: My lab is neuroimmunology and behavior where we are interested in how the peripheral nervous system and peripheral immune system interact.

codes.evidence: How we can contribute to the greater microbiome cause.

codes.evidence: Our lab is interested in designing fluorescent sensors to image the neuromodulators in the brain.

codes.evidence: We are now particularly interested in detecting opioid peptides.

codes.evidence: I'm a computational neuroscientist working on the intersection of AI and neuroscience.

codes.evidence: I lead a systems neuroscience lab really interested in understanding the biological correlates that drive the neuroimaging findings.
codes.explanation: JP shares his lab's focus on the biological correlates of neuroimaging findings.

codes.evidence: For all of these incredible works that all of you are doing, in order for us to really start to see these in our patients.
codes.explanation: JP uses joint framing to connect the group's work to clinical outcomes.

codes.evidence: I have this fantasy to combine this very basic science work I did in my early 20s with what I do now.
codes.explanation: Sarah shares her personal aspiration to bridge her past basic science work with her current clinical practice.

ideas_currently_on_table: Gut-brain axis interactions in cancer and chemotherapy
Proteomic and lipidomic profiling in neurodegeneration
Neuroimmunology and behavior in peripheral nervous system
Adaptive deep brain stimulation for Parkinson's
chunk_summaries: The participants introduced their diverse research backgrounds, ranging from neuroimmunology and mass spectrometry to computational neuroscience and clinical pediatrics. The tone was highly collaborative, with participants actively listening and finding common ground in the gut-brain axis. The group ended the chunk in a divergent phase, having established a shared interest in integrating their expertise to understand the biological basis of neuroimaging findings.
